mappings.rationaleAI: Core text covering ecosystems, communities, energy flow, biotic/abiotic components, and ecosystem productivity (A.2, A.3, B).
Frames the biosphere as an integrated system, encompassing biogeochemical cycles (carbon, nitrogen, water), energy flow, and the Gaia hypothesis (A.1, A.3, A.4).
Details atmospheric composition, the carbon/oxygen cycles, the greenhouse effect, and solar radiation as an abiotic component of ecosystems (A.4.b, B.3.b).
Explains how temperature, pressure, and weather patterns function as abiotic drivers shaping ecosystems, alongside the carbon dioxide cycle (B.3.a, A.4.b).
Explores the chemistry and physical properties of water, directly relevant to the water cycle and water as a key abiotic ecosystem component (A.4.e, B.3.d).
Highlights bacteria, archaea, and fungi as decomposers that drive nitrogen fixation and nutrient cycling in ecosystems (A.4.c, B.2.c).
Illustrates forest ecosystems as major biosphere components with carbon cycling, energy flow, and producer/consumer/decomposer trophic levels (A.3, B.2).
Presents oceanic environments as major biosphere components, including marine nutrient cycling and the ocean's role in the global water cycle (A.4.e, B.3.d).
Examines marine producers, consumers, and decomposers alongside the biotic components that structure ocean ecosystems (B.2).
Maps the distribution of life across levels of biological organization -- from populations to biomes -- throughout the biosphere (A.2).
Directly covers fire as a limiting factor in ecosystems and its role in shaping vegetation structure and nutrient turnover (B.3.f).
Explains mineral composition of soils and sedimentary processes relevant to the sedimentary cycles of essential minerals in ecosystems (A.4.f, B.3.d).
Addresses human transformation of the biosphere, directly relevant to the concept of the noosphere and conditioning of the abiotic environment by organisms (A.5, B.4).
Investigates the conditions necessary for life on Earth -- oxygen levels, carbon dioxide balance, and habitability -- as preconditions of the biosphere (A.1).
